research.title: Workflow Execution Engine — Option Exploration
research.topic: What architecture should the Workflow Execution Engine use to orchestrate multi-step AI processes?
research.summary: Custom event-sourced state machine using existing forge patterns (JSON files + Redis + AgentLoop) is the clear winner. External orchestrators (Temporal/Prefect) are overkill for minutes-long workflows on a desktop app. LangGraph doesn't provide workflow-level features needed.
key_findings: forge-api already has 80% of needed infrastructure: AgentLoop (LLM execution with pause/resume), EventBus (Redis pub/sub with 30+ event types), SessionManager (Redis session persistence), execution.py (async background tasks)
AgentLoop already supports blocking-decision pause/resume — the exact pattern needed for workflow step gates
forge-core commands (pipeline, decisions, changes, etc.) work via subprocess or direct Python import — both viable for engine integration
JSON file persistence in forge_output/ follows append-only pattern proven across all entity types — suitable for workflow state
No Postgres exists in the stack — adding it would be a significant infrastructure change for marginal benefit
Temporal/Prefect require external infrastructure and are designed for cloud-scale — overkill for a desktop orchestrator running 3-10 concurrent workflows
LangGraph is agent-graph focused but lacks workflow-level features (user decision gates, multi-session orchestration)
python-statemachine and transitions libraries provide clean FSM abstractions with async support — could reduce boilerplate
Existing execution.py has mock-only execution — needs full rewrite to support real LLM agent execution per step

research.title: Workflow Execution Engine — Architecture Design
research.topic: How to architect the workflow engine: components, data model, integration points, step types
research.summary: 5-component architecture: WorkflowDefinition (step DAG schema), WorkflowEngine (async orchestrator), StepExecutor (per-type runners wrapping AgentLoop), WorkflowStore (JSON persistence), WorkflowRouter (REST+WS endpoints). Steps typed as llm_agent, forge_command, user_decision, conditional, parallel_group.
key_findings: 5-component architecture: WorkflowDefinition, WorkflowEngine, StepExecutor, WorkflowStore, WorkflowRouter
5 step types: llm_agent (AgentLoop), forge_command (subprocess), user_decision (pause+notify), conditional (routing), parallel_group (concurrent)
JSON file persistence (workflows.json) — consistent with all other forge state, no new infrastructure
Per-step LLM sessions (not shared) to prevent context overflow — previous step summaries injected as context
asyncio.Task per execution — lightweight concurrency for 3+ parallel workflows
Subprocess for forge-core commands — clean separation, same interface as CLI
6 key integration points with existing forge-api modules (AgentLoop, EventBus, SessionManager, ContextResolver, ToolRegistry, execution.py)
7 new files to create in forge-api/app/workflow/ package

research.title: Workflow Execution Engine — Risk Assessment
research.topic: What risks does building the workflow execution engine carry and how to mitigate them?
research.summary: 6 risks identified. Top risks: workflow state corruption on concurrent writes (MEDIUM — mitigated by atomic writes), stuck workflows without timeouts (HIGH — mitigated by per-step timeouts), and complexity creep making the engine unmaintainable (MEDIUM — mitigated by strict component separation). No critical/blocking risks — GO verdict.
key_findings: 6 risks identified, no critical blockers — GO verdict for implementation
Top risk: workflow stuck forever (composite 21) — mitigated by per-step timeouts and health checks
Complexity creep is the strategic risk — mitigated by strict phased implementation (linear -> conditional -> parallel)
Redis failure cascades to both events and sessions — need graceful degradation
State corruption mitigated by atomic writes (proven forge-core pattern) and per-execution file locking
No Cobra Effects in proposed mitigations

research.title: Notification Infrastructure Option Exploration
research.topic: How to build Decision & Notification Infrastructure on existing WebSocket + Redis Pub/Sub
research.summary: Three key architectural decisions explored: notification persistence (recommend hybrid Redis Streams + entity), notification backend architecture (recommend dedicated notification entity), and notification UI (recommend enhanced bell + detail views). Existing infrastructure covers ~40% of needs.
key_findings: Existing wsDispatcher already routes decision.created/closed events to notificationStore - foundation exists
NotificationCenter polls every 30s instead of using WebSocket - easy win to convert
Redis Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget - notifications for offline users require persistence layer
Decision notification popup already handles single-notification queue - needs multi-notification support
LLM session pause/resume on decision close already wired in wsDispatcher - needs notification integration
Store factory pattern (createEntityStore) can generate notificationStore with minimal code
Cross-project notifications need either global WebSocket channel or polling fallback
Notification volume under concurrent workflows could cause UI performance issues without throttling

research.title: Entity Management via AI Sidebar - Option Exploration
research.topic: How to achieve full entity management, slash command workflow sessions, real-time updates, and multi-turn context in forge-web AI sidebar
research.summary: Infrastructure is 80% built. KR-3 (real-time) nearly complete. Key gaps: slash command workflow sessions (KR-2), tool coverage holes (KR-1 DELETE ops, AC templates), multi-turn token management (KR-4). Recommended: incremental enhancement approach, not rebuild.
key_findings: 42 tools already registered covering 12 entity types - most CRUD operations work
DELETE operations missing for ALL entity types - only status-based archival exists
AC templates lack create/update tools - only instantiate available
Slash commands inject text only - no session_type routing or workflow-specific tool filtering
SWR revalidation pipeline ALREADY WORKS: wsDispatcher -> TOOL_TO_ENTITY mapping -> debounced mutate() -> toast
MutationTracker (5s TTL) prevents WS echo double-processing
Multi-turn sessions persist in Redis (24h TTL) but no token budget management or history summarization
Session types (chat/plan/execute/verify/compound) stored but not behavior-changing
Page context annotation system mature - register/unregister/serialize to SKILL format
Scope negotiation with [suggest-scope:X] pattern working end-to-end

research.title: Notification Infrastructure Risk Assessment
research.topic: Risks of building Decision & Notification Infrastructure on existing WebSocket + Redis stack
research.summary: 8 risks identified, 2 HIGH composite (notification flood under concurrency, WebSocket reconnection gap). Key mitigation: server-side persistence + throttling + reconnect fetch. No blockers - all risks manageable with standard patterns.
key_findings: Notification flood under concurrent workflows is highest risk (composite 18) - needs server-side throttling/batching
WebSocket reconnection gap can lose notifications (composite 16) - mitigated by fetch-on-reconnect from persistent store
Cross-project notification routing has no existing infrastructure (composite 14) - needs global channel or polling
Notification entity file locking under concurrent writes is medium risk - existing asyncio.Lock pattern sufficient
No Cobra effects detected - mitigations are straightforward and well-understood patterns
Biggest uncertainty: actual notification volume under real concurrent workflows

research.title: Notification Infrastructure Architecture Design
research.topic: Architecture for Decision & Notification system on existing WebSocket + Redis Pub/Sub
research.summary: 6-component architecture: NotificationEntity (storage), NotificationService (event mapping), NotificationRouter (REST API), NotificationStore (frontend state), NotificationCenter (enhanced UI), ResponseModals (per-type detail views). 4 ADRs recorded. Uses existing patterns throughout - factory store, entity router, WebSocket events.
key_findings: Architecture requires 6 components across 3 layers: storage, service, and presentation
All components follow existing Forge patterns - no new architectural concepts needed
Notification lifecycle: EVENT -> NotificationService (filter/enrich) -> storage + WS emit -> frontend store -> UI
4 notification types with distinct response UIs: decision, approval, question, alert
Priority system (critical/high/normal/low) drives display behavior: critical=popup, normal=bell count
ADR-1: Server-side persistence over client-only (for zero-miss guarantee KR-4)
ADR-2: Single notification entity over separate per-type entities (simplicity)
ADR-3: Response modals over navigation (avoid context switching)
ADR-4: Workflow tagging over separate channels (reuse existing WS connection)

research.title: Entity Management via AI Sidebar - Feasibility Assessment
research.topic: Is full entity management via AI sidebar feasible within medium appetite (weeks)?
research.summary: CONDITIONAL GO (avg 3.8/5). Infrastructure 80% built makes this highly feasible technically. Binding concern: LLM tool-call accuracy for 95% success rate target is unvalidated. Conditions: (1) measure baseline tool-call accuracy before committing to KR-1 target, (2) verify entity WS event emission from LLM tool handlers.
key_findings: Technical feasibility HIGH (4/5) - 42 tools exist, SWR pipeline works, skill injection built
Resource feasibility HIGH (4/5) - single developer can implement incrementally
Knowledge feasibility HIGH (4/5) - existing codebase patterns well-established (factory, SWR, Zustand)
Temporal feasibility MEDIUM (3/5) - medium appetite (weeks) is realistic for incremental approach, tight for full 95% target
Compositional feasibility HIGH (4/5) - all parts already integrate, just gaps to fill
Dependency feasibility MEDIUM (3/5) - LLM provider tool-calling quality is external dependency
Planning fallacy: comparable projects (Cursor chat, Copilot workspace) took 3-6 months - but they built from scratch, we enhance existing
Most optimistic assumption: LLM will achieve 95% tool-call accuracy with improved descriptions alone

research.title: Entity Management via AI Sidebar - Risk Assessment
research.topic: What are the risks of implementing full entity management via AI sidebar?
research.summary: 8 risks identified. Top risk: LLM tool confusion with 42+ tools (composite 18). Second: data loss from DELETE operations (composite 16). Third: context window exhaustion in multi-turn (composite 15). All mitigable. No show-stoppers.
key_findings: RISK-1 (composite 18): LLM tool confusion - 42+ tools may cause wrong tool selection. Mitigation: scope-based filtering limits visible tools to 8-12 per context
RISK-2 (composite 16): Data loss from DELETE tools - users may accidentally delete entities via natural language. Mitigation: use archive/remove semantics, add confirmation step
RISK-3 (composite 15): Context window exhaustion - multi-turn sessions accumulate tokens. Mitigation: hybrid pinning + sliding window (KR-4 Option C)
RISK-4 (composite 14): Slash command ambiguity - /plan could mean create plan OR show plan. Mitigation: skill SKILL.md provides disambiguation context to LLM
RISK-5 (composite 13): SWR revalidation race - tool mutation completes but SWR cache serves stale data. Mitigation: existing 300ms debounce + MutationTracker handles this
RISK-6 (composite 12): Workflow state drift - LLM deviates from skill instructions mid-workflow. Mitigation: session workflow state tracking
RISK-7 (composite 11): Token cost explosion - multi-turn with tool calls = 5-10x cost per conversation. Mitigation: token budget per session, user-visible cost
RISK-8 (composite 10): Skill injection context bloat - 5 skills x 16KB = 80KB extra context. Mitigation: existing budget (30KB total) already enforced

research.title: Entity Management via AI Sidebar - Architecture Design
research.topic: How should the AI sidebar architecture evolve to support full entity management, slash command workflows, real-time updates, and multi-turn context?
research.summary: Extend existing 4-layer architecture with 3 new components: (1) Slash Command Router mapping /commands to skill injection + session_type, (2) Workflow State Machine on sessions for multi-step workflow enforcement, (3) Context Window Manager with hybrid pinning + sliding window. All additive changes - no restructuring needed.
key_findings: Existing 4-layer architecture (app context, entity context, page context, skill context) is sound - extend, do not replace
ADR-1: Slash Command Router - frontend-side mapping of /command to skill name + session_type + initial tool hint
ADR-2: Workflow State Machine - session-level state tracking (current_step, completed_steps, expected_tools) for multi-step workflows
ADR-3: Context Window Manager - hybrid approach: pin tool results + session summary, sliding window for conversation messages
ADR-4: Entity Inline Cards - chat message post-processor that detects entity references (T-001, D-003) and renders clickable cards
ADR-5: Tool Coverage Completion - archive/remove tools instead of hard DELETE, matching Forge append-only semantics
No new services or infrastructure needed - all changes are within existing forge-api and forge-web codebases
C4 Component: SlashCommandRouter (FE) -> SkillInjector (BE) -> AgentLoop (BE) -> ToolRegistry (BE) -> wsDispatcher (FE) -> SWR
